title: Fibonacci (memoized recursion)
code: public static long FibMemo(int n, Dictionary<int, long>? memo = null)
if (memo.TryGetValue(n, out long cached)) return cached;
long result = FibMemo(n - 1, memo) + FibMemo(n - 2, memo);

memo.label: memo[k] = Fib(k) once computed ( · = not yet cached )

stack.label: call stack: Fib(n) frames in flight

frames.caption: Fib(6): memo MISS → recurse Fib(5) + Fib(4).
frames.note: Memo MISS: this Fib(n) has never been computed, so there is nothing cached. We push a new call frame and recurse into Fib(n-1) and Fib(n-2). This is the only place real work happens — and because we cache afterwards, it happens at most ONCE per distinct n.

frames.caption: Fib(5): memo MISS → recurse Fib(4) + Fib(3).
frames.note: Memo MISS: this Fib(n) has never been computed, so there is nothing cached. We push a new call frame and recurse into Fib(n-1) and Fib(n-2). This is the only place real work happens — and because we cache afterwards, it happens at most ONCE per distinct n.

frames.caption: Fib(4): memo MISS → recurse Fib(3) + Fib(2).
frames.note: Memo MISS: this Fib(n) has never been computed, so there is nothing cached. We push a new call frame and recurse into Fib(n-1) and Fib(n-2). This is the only place real work happens — and because we cache afterwards, it happens at most ONCE per distinct n.

frames.caption: Fib(3): memo MISS → recurse Fib(2) + Fib(1).
frames.note: Memo MISS: this Fib(n) has never been computed, so there is nothing cached. We push a new call frame and recurse into Fib(n-1) and Fib(n-2). This is the only place real work happens — and because we cache afterwards, it happens at most ONCE per distinct n.

frames.caption: Fib(2): memo MISS → recurse Fib(1) + Fib(0).
frames.note: Memo MISS: this Fib(n) has never been computed, so there is nothing cached. We push a new call frame and recurse into Fib(n-1) and Fib(n-2). This is the only place real work happens — and because we cache afterwards, it happens at most ONCE per distinct n.

frames.caption: Fib(1): n < 2, base case → return 1.
frames.note: Base case: Fib(0)=0 and Fib(1)=1 are returned directly without recursing or caching. These two seeds are where the recursion bottoms out — every larger value is ultimately built from them. Without a base case the recursion would never stop.

frames.caption: Fib(0): n < 2, base case → return 0.
frames.note: Base case: Fib(0)=0 and Fib(1)=1 are returned directly without recursing or caching. These two seeds are where the recursion bottoms out — every larger value is ultimately built from them. Without a base case the recursion would never stop.

frames.caption: Fib(2) = 1 + 0 = 1. Store memo[2] = 1, then return.
frames.note: Both children have returned, so result = Fib(n-1) + Fib(n-2). We store it in memo[n] before returning, pop this frame off the stack, and unwind. From now on any other call that needs Fib(n) gets the cached value — this write is what makes the next request a HIT.

frames.caption: Fib(1): n < 2, base case → return 1.
frames.note: Base case: Fib(0)=0 and Fib(1)=1 are returned directly without recursing or caching. These two seeds are where the recursion bottoms out — every larger value is ultimately built from them. Without a base case the recursion would never stop.

frames.caption: Fib(3) = 1 + 1 = 2. Store memo[3] = 2, then return.
frames.note: Both children have returned, so result = Fib(n-1) + Fib(n-2). We store it in memo[n] before returning, pop this frame off the stack, and unwind. From now on any other call that needs Fib(n) gets the cached value — this write is what makes the next request a HIT.

frames.caption: Fib(2): memo HIT → return cached 1 (whole subtree skipped).
frames.note: Memo HIT: Fib(n) is already in the cache, so we return the stored value instantly (an O(1) lookup) instead of re-expanding its whole subtree. This single check is what turns the exponential O(2^n) recomputation into a linear O(n) walk — the overlapping subproblems are computed once and reused.

frames.caption: Fib(4) = 2 + 1 = 3. Store memo[4] = 3, then return.
frames.note: Both children have returned, so result = Fib(n-1) + Fib(n-2). We store it in memo[n] before returning, pop this frame off the stack, and unwind. From now on any other call that needs Fib(n) gets the cached value — this write is what makes the next request a HIT.

frames.caption: Fib(3): memo HIT → return cached 2 (whole subtree skipped).
frames.note: Memo HIT: Fib(n) is already in the cache, so we return the stored value instantly (an O(1) lookup) instead of re-expanding its whole subtree. This single check is what turns the exponential O(2^n) recomputation into a linear O(n) walk — the overlapping subproblems are computed once and reused.

frames.caption: Fib(5) = 3 + 2 = 5. Store memo[5] = 5, then return.
frames.note: Both children have returned, so result = Fib(n-1) + Fib(n-2). We store it in memo[n] before returning, pop this frame off the stack, and unwind. From now on any other call that needs Fib(n) gets the cached value — this write is what makes the next request a HIT.

frames.caption: Fib(4): memo HIT → return cached 3 (whole subtree skipped).
frames.note: Memo HIT: Fib(n) is already in the cache, so we return the stored value instantly (an O(1) lookup) instead of re-expanding its whole subtree. This single check is what turns the exponential O(2^n) recomputation into a linear O(n) walk — the overlapping subproblems are computed once and reused.

frames.caption: Fib(6) = 5 + 3 = 8. Store memo[6] = 8, then return.
frames.note: Both children have returned, so result = Fib(n-1) + Fib(n-2). We store it in memo[n] before returning, pop this frame off the stack, and unwind. From now on any other call that needs Fib(n) gets the cached value — this write is what makes the next request a HIT.

frames.caption: Done: Fib(6) = 8. Only 11 calls — every distinct Fib(k) was computed once and cached.
frames.note: Compare with naive tree recursion, which would make 25 calls for Fib(6) (and O(2^n) in general). Memoization caches each of the n+1 subproblems on first computation, so the total work is O(n) time and O(n) space (the cache plus the O(n)-deep stack). This caching of overlapping subproblems is exactly the bridge from recursion to dynamic programming.
